Here's something that most people don't know. Airports are technically one of the most dangerous places for officers to make arrests because of the federal jurisdictional mess that hangs over every square inch. You got TSA, airport police, city cops, and federal marshals all potentially involved, but rarely coordinated. That means when someone calls the problem inside an airport, officers don't just have to worry about public safety. They have to worry about stepping on the wrong agency's toes. Think about it, families, pilots, unarmed TSA agents and flight crews are one wrong step away from mayhem. So cops walk into this mess with one job, shut it down before it spirals. But when the suspect's already deep inside a spiral of rage, the only option is to contain it and fast. And if you watch closely, you'll notice something during these kinds of calls because the eyes of the suspect often don't match their surroundings. It's like they're in a different world, still fighting their demons. And some people don't want a way out. They want a target. You could show up calm, patient and respectful and it wouldn't matter. Because for certain individuals, especially being emotionally unstable, confrontation is the goal. I've lost count of how many times I started out respectful, calm voice, open posture, trying to deescalate like we're trained, and minutes later, I'm on the ground fighting to keep someone from grabbing my gun or headbutting me in front of everybody watching. But here's the problem, on many occasions, once the cuffs are on, the phones come out and suddenly the officer is the problem, not the guy screaming at strangers at an airport terminal, but the person in uniform. And in today's world, the more someone resists, the more people question the one who stood firm. But if we keep confusing professionalism with weakness, one day the people who run towards these calls might just stop showing up. And trust me, we don't want that. This suspect was charged with resisting with violence, resisting without violence, assault on a law enforcement officer and disorderly conduct, totaling a bond of $5,500.
